food_name: Braised Ee-Fu Noodle with Three Mushrooms (Large)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ee-Fu (Yi Mein) noodle dish; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03315571) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC 'Pasta and noodles, cooked' is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for braised Ee-Fu noodle exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Ee-Fu noodle or Yi Mein dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large restaurant serving of braised Ee-Fu noodle intended for 4–6 people, weighing approximately 800 g in total, or roughly 160–200 g per person.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles, egg, cooked / Noodles, Chinese, chow mein, dry — adapted composite estimate for braised Ee-Fu noodle
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) supplemented by HPB Singapore Foodo analogous noodle dish data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Ee-Fu (Yi Mein) noodles are deep-fried alkaline wheat noodles that are then braised. No exact entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for braised Ee-Fu noodle as a complete dish. Values are estimated from: (1) USDA SR Legacy item 20109 'Noodles, Chinese, chow mein, dry' for the noodle base macros, adjusted downward for water uptake during braising; (2) HPB Singapore Foodo analogous braised noodle dish entries for sodium and fat contribution from sauce; (3) FAO/INFOODS SEA mushroom data for fibre contribution. The composite estimate reflects a typical restaurant preparation with oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, and three varieties of mushroom. Per-100 g values represent the finished braised dish.

gi_source.title: Noodles, instant, boiled — GI 47 (±5); Wheat noodles, boiled — GI 53; composite analogous estimate for braised Ee-Fu noodle
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised Ee-Fu (Yi Mein) noodle. Ee-Fu noodles are alkaline wheat noodles that are pre-fried then braised; the alkaline processing and dense texture lower the GI relative to plain boiled wheat noodles. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values of 47 for instant noodles (boiled) and 53 for wheat noodles (boiled). A midpoint estimate of GI 50 is used, consistent with the dense, braised texture of Ee-Fu noodles and the moderating effect of sauce, mushrooms, and fat on glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 36) / 100 = 18.0, based on a 160 g individual serving containing approximately 36 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from oyster sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 160 g individual serving of braised Ee-Fu noodle contributes approximately 672 mg sodium, representing over 29% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined wheat noodle base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 50 (low) and a glycemic load of 18 per individual serving, braised Ee-Fu noodle produces a moderate glycemic response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables or protein to further blunt postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from sauce and pre-frying of noodles
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate fat from the pre-fried noodle base and oyster sauce are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions and choose preparations with reduced soy and oyster sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Ee-Fu Noodle with Three Mushrooms (三菇伊面, large) is a classic Cantonese noodle dish widely served at Chinese seafood restaurants in Singapore. Ee-Fu noodles, also known as Yi Mein or Yee Mee, are thick, golden-yellow alkaline wheat noodles that are deep-fried during manufacture, giving them a distinctive spongy, porous texture that readily absorbs braising sauces. In this preparation, the noodles are braised in a savoury sauce made from oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and chicken or vegetable stock, and combined with three varieties of mushroom — typically shiitake, wood ear, and straw mushrooms — which contribute umami depth, dietary fibre, and a range of textures. The large portion is designed for sharing among 4–6 people. The finished dish has a glossy, amber-coloured sauce coating the noodles, with a soft yet slightly chewy bite. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 148 kcal, 4.8 g protein, 4.2 g fat, and 22.5 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of around 420 mg per 100 g due to the sauce components. The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low), making it a moderate glycemic-load dish per individual serving. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the moderate glycemic load warrants portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-suitable when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.

What mushrooms are in three mushroom Yi Mein?

meal_context: Lunch or dinner staple at Chinese zi char restaurants in Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact entry for braised Ee-Fu noodle as a complete dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy noodle entries and HPB analogous braised noodle dish data, adjusted for the braising process and sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using wheat noodle and instant noodle reference values. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry covers braised Ee-Fu noodle as a complete dish. Values should be updated if HPB Singapore Foodo publishes a direct entry. 2. Sodium content is particularly variable depending on the amount of oyster sauce and soy sauce used; the estimate of 420 mg/100 g reflects a typical restaurant preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as Ee-Fu noodles are commercially produced using vegetable oils and trans fat content is negligible per current manufacturing standards; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy analogous noodle data. 4. cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0 as this dish in its standard three-mushroom preparation contains no animal-derived ingredients beyond stock; confirmed zero for the mushroom-only variant. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy noodle entries used as the primary analogue. 6. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as this dish is plant-forward and B12 is not reported in the source data. 8. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data. 12. The large (L) portion designation from field survey refers to a shared serving for 4–6 pax at SGD 26.00; individual per-person nutritional values should be calculated by dividing the total dish weight by the number of diners. 13. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation without pork; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant.
